items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.misconception: Six beats per bar, all equal in stress.
items.why: Students count 6/8 as six equal beats. In practice it's felt as TWO dotted-crotchet beats subdivided into three quavers each - compound duple, not simple sextuple.

items.q: How do you identify a major key from its key signature?
items.correct: The last sharp is the leading note (semitone below tonic); the second-to-last flat is the tonic.
items.misconception: The number of sharps or flats equals the tonic note number.
items.why: Students lack a reliable rule. For sharp keys: last sharp + 1 semitone = tonic (e.g., F sharp means G major). For flat keys: the second-to-last flat IS the tonic (or memorise: 1 flat = F major).

items.q: Which orchestral family does the saxophone belong to?
items.misconception: Brass (because it is made of brass).
items.why: Students classify by material. Instrument families are defined by SOUND PRODUCTION: woodwinds use a reed or air across an edge. The sax uses a reed, so despite the brass body, it's woodwind.

items.q: Middle C in alto clef sits on the:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Middle line. “Top line” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Compound. “Simple” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many beats per bar in 9/8 (compound)?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
